• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Psychometric Analysis: IIC, Reliability, EFA, CFA

AI-Generated Resolution Scales & Zhang et al. (2025) Criterion Scale

Kentaro Suzuki

2026-03-08

1 Introduction

This report presents a comprehensive psychometric evaluation of six AI-generated Resolution scales and the Zhang et al. (2025) criterion measure of trust in AI. The study employs a 2 (LLM: GPT-5, Google Gemini Flash 2.5) × 3 (Prompt Strategy: Zero-Shot, Few-Shot-Basic, Few-Shot-Advanced) between-subjects design, producing six AI-generated scales. Each AI-generated scale contains six items rated on a 7-point Likert scale. The criterion scale (Zhang et al., 2025) contains 18 items distributed across three subscales: Interaction (k = 11), Truth (k = 4), and Resolution (k = 3).

The analyses proceed in four stages: (1) inter-item correlation analysis to assess item-level relationships and identify problematic items, (2) reliability analysis using Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega, (3) exploratory factor analysis (EFA) to examine dimensionality, and (4) confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to test hypothesized factor structures. For the criterion scale, all analyses employ multiple imputation (m = 20) with results pooled via Rubin’s rules.

2 Inter-Item Correlations (IIC)

2.1 AI Scales

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  d <- iic_details[[nm]]
  cat(sprintf("\n### %s (*n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", nm, d$n, d$k))
  cat("```\n")
  cat(sprintf("  Mean IIC = %s  |  SD = %s  |  Range: [%s, %s]\n",
              fmt(d$mean_iic), fmt(d$sd_iic), fmt(d$min_iic), fmt(d$max_iic)))

  if (nrow(d$weak) > 0) {
    cat("  FLAGGED WEAK (< .15):\n")
    for (row_i in seq_len(nrow(d$weak)))
      cat(sprintf("    %s -- %s: r = %s\n",
                  d$items[d$weak[row_i, 1]], d$items[d$weak[row_i, 2]],
                  fmt(d$corr_mat[d$weak[row_i, 1], d$weak[row_i, 2]])))
  }
  if (nrow(d$vhigh) > 0) {
    cat("  FLAGGED VERY HIGH (> .80):\n")
    for (row_i in seq_len(nrow(d$vhigh)))
      cat(sprintf("    %s -- %s: r = %s\n",
                  d$items[d$vhigh[row_i, 1]], d$items[d$vhigh[row_i, 2]],
                  fmt(d$corr_mat[d$vhigh[row_i, 1], d$vhigh[row_i, 2]])))
  }

  if (grepl("Google Zero-Shot", nm)) {
    idx_r <- which(d$items == "Res_Item3_R")
    cat("  [FLAGGED: Res_Item3_R is reverse-coded (already reverse-scored).]\n")
    cat("  Res_Item3_R pairwise correlations:\n")
    for (j in seq_len(d$k)) {
      if (j != idx_r)
        cat(sprintf("    %s -- Res_Item3_R: r = %s\n",
                    d$items[j], fmt(d$corr_mat[j, idx_r])))
    }
  }

  print_iic_matrix(d$corr_mat, d$means, d$sds, d$items, nm)
  cat("```\n\n")
}

2.1.7 AI Scales — Inter-Item Correlation Heatmap

all_mirror <- data.frame()

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  dat_i <- ai_data[[nm]]
  cm <- cor(dat_i, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
  std_labels <- paste("Item", 1:6)
  rownames(cm) <- colnames(cm) <- std_labels

  df <- as.data.frame(cm)
  df$Var1 <- factor(std_labels, levels = rev(std_labels))
  long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = -Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "r")
  long$Var2 <- factor(long$Var2, levels = std_labels)
  long$r_fill  <- ifelse(long$Var1 == long$Var2, 1.0, long$r)
  long$r_label <- ifelse(long$Var1 == long$Var2, "", sprintf("%.2f", long$r))
  long$Scale <- nm
  all_mirror <- rbind(all_mirror, long)
}

all_mirror$Scale <- factor(all_mirror$Scale, levels = c(
  "GPT5 Zero-Shot", "GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic", "GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced",
  "Google Zero-Shot", "Google Few-Shot-Basic", "Google Few-Shot-Advanced"))
all_mirror$text_col <- ifelse(all_mirror$r_fill > 0.7, "white", "grey20")

anno_cells <- all_mirror[all_mirror$Scale == "Google Zero-Shot" &
                           all_mirror$Var1 != all_mirror$Var2 &
                           (all_mirror$Var1 == "Item 3" | all_mirror$Var2 == "Item 3"), ]

anno_label <- data.frame(
  Var2  = factor("Item 5", levels = levels(all_mirror$Var2)),
  Var1  = factor("Item 1", levels = levels(all_mirror$Var1)),
  Scale = factor("Google Zero-Shot", levels = levels(all_mirror$Scale)),
  label = "Item 3 = Res_Item3_R\n(reverse-coded;\nnear-zero correlations)")

p_ai <- ggplot(all_mirror, aes(Var2, Var1, fill = r_fill)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_tile(data = anno_cells, fill = NA, color = "#B2182B", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = r_label, color = text_col), size = 2.3) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  geom_label(data = anno_label, aes(x = Var2, y = Var1, label = label),
             fill = "white", color = "#B2182B", size = 2.2,
             fontface = "italic", label.padding = unit(0.2, "lines"),
             label.size = 0.3, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colours = c("#FFFFFF", "#C6DBEF", "#6BAED6", "#2171B5", "#08306B"),
    values  = c(0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0),
    limits  = c(-0.1, 1),
    name    = expression(italic(r))) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scale, ncol = 3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       title = "Inter-Item Correlation Matrices Across AI-Generated Resolution Scales",
       caption = "Note. Red-bordered cells in the Google Zero-Shot panel indicate Res_Item3_R (reverse-coded, already reverse-scored), which shows near-zero correlations with all other items.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid  = element_blank(),
    strip.text  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 7.5, face = "italic"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm"))

p_ai

Figure 1. Inter-item correlation matrices across the six AI-generated Resolution scales. Correlation coefficients are displayed in each cell, with colour intensity representing the magnitude of the association. Red-bordered cells in the Google Zero-Shot panel highlight Res_Item3_R (reverse-coded), which exhibits near-zero correlations with all other items. 

ggsave(file.path(OUT_DIR, "heatmap_AI_faceted_mirrored.png"),
       p_ai, width = 11, height = 7.5, dpi = 250)
ggsave(file.path(OUT_DIR, "heatmap_AI_faceted_mirrored.pdf"),
       p_ai, width = 11, height = 7.5, device = cairo_pdf)

Inter-item correlation (IIC) matrices were computed for each of the six AI-generated Resolution scales. Across the five scales that did not contain a reverse-coded item, mean inter-item correlations fell within the recommended range of .15 to .50 (Clark & Watson, 1995), suggesting adequate item homogeneity without excessive redundancy. As shown in Figure 1, the GPT-5 and Google Few-Shot conditions generally produced cohesive item sets with moderate-to-strong positive inter-item associations.

A notable exception was the Google Zero-Shot scale, which included the reverse-coded item Res_Item3_R. This item produced near-zero correlations with all other items in the set (see red-bordered cells in Figure 1), indicating that it did not function coherently within the unidimensional scale. This pattern suggests either a method effect associated with reverse-coded wording or a substantive misalignment between the reverse-coded item content and the remaining positively worded items.

In Simple Terms. The IIC analysis checks whether items on the same scale are related to each other — they should be correlated, but not so highly correlated that they are essentially asking the same question. Most of the AI-generated scales showed healthy inter-item relationships. The one problem case was the Google Zero-Shot scale, where Item 3 (which was worded in the opposite direction) did not correlate with any of the other items. This means respondents who scored high on the other five items did not necessarily score in the expected direction on Item 3, suggesting that item does not belong with the rest.

Implications. The near-zero correlations for Res_Item3_R in the Google Zero-Shot condition raise a methodological concern about the use of reverse-coded items in AI-generated scales. If this item were retained, it would attenuate internal consistency and distort factor solutions. Researchers using AI-generated items should evaluate whether reverse-coded items generated by LLMs are substantively equivalent to their positively worded counterparts or whether they introduce construct-irrelevant variance. This finding also supports the broader psychometric literature cautioning against the inclusion of reverse-worded items in short scales (e.g., Weijters et al., 2013).

2.2 Criterion Subscales (Pooled, m = 20)

if (has_imputed) {
  for (sub_nm in names(cri_subscales)) {
    d <- cri_iic_details[[sub_nm]]
    cat(sprintf("\n### %s (avg *n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", sub_nm, d$n, d$k))
    cat("```\n")
    cat(sprintf("  Mean IIC = %s  |  SD = %s  |  Range: [%s, %s]\n",
                fmt(d$mean_iic), fmt(d$sd_iic), fmt(d$min_iic), fmt(d$max_iic)))

    if (nrow(d$weak) > 0) {
      cat("  FLAGGED WEAK (< .15):\n")
      for (row_i in seq_len(nrow(d$weak)))
        cat(sprintf("    %s -- %s: r = %s\n",
                    d$items[d$weak[row_i, 1]], d$items[d$weak[row_i, 2]],
                    fmt(d$corr_mat[d$weak[row_i, 1], d$weak[row_i, 2]])))
    }
    if (nrow(d$vhigh) > 0) {
      cat("  FLAGGED VERY HIGH (> .80):\n")
      for (row_i in seq_len(nrow(d$vhigh)))
        cat(sprintf("    %s -- %s: r = %s\n",
                    d$items[d$vhigh[row_i, 1]], d$items[d$vhigh[row_i, 2]],
                    fmt(d$corr_mat[d$vhigh[row_i, 1], d$vhigh[row_i, 2]])))
    }

    print_iic_matrix(d$corr_mat, d$means, d$sds, d$items, sub_nm)
    cat("```\n\n")
  }
}

2.2.1 Criterion: Interaction (avg n = 1222, k = 11)

  Mean IIC = 0.374  |  SD = 0.185  |  Range: [0.126, 0.725]
  FLAGGED WEAK (< .15):
    CF_CRI_TRU.7 -- CF_CRI_TRU.0: r = 0.126

2.2.5 Criterion Subscales — Inter-Item Correlation Heatmaps

if (has_imputed) {
  for (sub_nm in names(cri_subscales)) {
    sub_items <- cri_subscales[[sub_nm]]
    corr_list <- list()
    for (m in seq_len(M_IMPUTATIONS)) {
      d <- imputed_dfs[[m]][, sub_items, drop = FALSE]
      d <- d[complete.cases(d), , drop = FALSE]
      corr_list[[m]] <- cor(d, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
    }
    pooled <- pool_corr_fisher(corr_list)
    short <- gsub("CF_CRI_TRU\\.", "", sub_items)
    rownames(pooled) <- colnames(pooled) <- short
    k <- length(short)

    df <- as.data.frame(pooled)
    df$Var1 <- factor(short, levels = rev(short))
    long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = -Var1, names_to = "Var2", values_to = "r")
    long$Var2 <- factor(long$Var2, levels = short)
    long$r_fill  <- ifelse(long$Var1 == long$Var2, 1.0, long$r)
    long$r_label <- ifelse(long$Var1 == long$Var2, "", sprintf("%.2f", long$r))
    long$text_col <- ifelse(long$r_fill > 0.7, "white", "grey20")

    clean_name <- gsub("Criterion: ", "", sub_nm)

    pi <- ggplot(long, aes(Var2, Var1, fill = r_fill)) +
      geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
      geom_text(aes(label = r_label, color = text_col),
                size = ifelse(k > 10, 2.2, 3.2)) +
      scale_color_identity() +
      scale_fill_gradientn(
        colours = c("#FFFFFF", "#C6DBEF", "#6BAED6", "#2171B5", "#08306B"),
        values  = c(0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0),
        limits  = c(-0.1, 1),
        name    = expression(italic(r))) +
      labs(title = paste0("Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: ", clean_name, " (Pooled, m = 20)"),
           x = NULL, y = NULL,
           caption = "Note. Item numbers correspond to CF_CRI_TRU indices. Correlations pooled via Fisher z-transformation.") +
      theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
      theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
            panel.grid = element_blank(),
            plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
            plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 7.5, face = "italic"),
            legend.key.height = unit(1.2, "cm"))

    print(pi)
    cat("\n\n")

    w <- ifelse(k > 10, 11, 6)
    h <- ifelse(k > 10, 9.5, 5.5)
    file_tag <- gsub("[: ]", "_", sub_nm)
    ggsave(file.path(OUT_DIR, sprintf("heatmap_%s.png", file_tag)),
           pi, width = w, height = h, dpi = 250)
    ggsave(file.path(OUT_DIR, sprintf("heatmap_%s.pdf", file_tag)),
           pi, width = w, height = h, device = cairo_pdf)
  }
}

Pooled inter-item correlation matrices were computed for each of the four criterion subscales (Interaction, Truth, Resolution, and the full 18-item scale) using Fisher’s z-transformation across m = 20 multiply imputed datasets. The Interaction subscale (k = 11) exhibited a broad range of inter-item correlations, consistent with a multifaceted factor tapping several aspects of conversational AI trust. The Truth subscale (k = 4) and Resolution subscale (k = 3) demonstrated more compact correlation ranges, as expected for shorter, more narrowly defined subscales. The full 18-item criterion scale showed a wider spread of correlations reflecting its multidimensional nature. Any item pairs flagged for unusually weak (r < .15) or very high (r > .80) correlations are noted in the matrices above.

In Simple Terms. These correlation matrices show how strongly each item on the Zhang et al. (2025) criterion scale relates to every other item on the same subscale. Because the criterion data had missing values, the analysis was run 20 times on different imputed datasets and the results were averaged. A well-functioning subscale shows moderate positive correlations among all its items — not too low (which would mean items are measuring different things) and not too high (which would mean items are redundant). The heatmaps provide a visual summary: darker blue cells indicate stronger associations.

Implications. The IIC patterns for the criterion subscales establish the baseline quality of the comparison measure. If the criterion subscales themselves show coherent inter-item structures, they provide a trustworthy benchmark against which the AI-generated scales can be evaluated. Any structural weaknesses in the criterion (e.g., item pairs with very low correlations) should be considered when interpreting convergent validity evidence in later analyses.

2.3 IIC Summary

iic_df <- do.call(rbind, iic_rows)
write.csv(iic_df, file.path(OUT_DIR, "iic_summary.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
iic_df
Scale n mean_IIC SD_IIC min_IIC max_IIC MI_Pooled
GPT5 Zero-Shot GPT5 Zero-Shot 202 0.4918762 0.1536080 0.2500229 0.7063619 No
GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic 186 0.5493415 0.0833805 0.3959943 0.6468966 No
GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced 216 0.6095372 0.0861849 0.5022379 0.7536531 No
Google Zero-Shot Google Zero-Shot 185 0.3735765 0.2330322 -0.0138816 0.7108059 No
Google Few-Shot-Basic Google Few-Shot-Basic 216 0.4316955 0.0879778 0.3223662 0.6091470 No
Google Few-Shot-Advanced Google Few-Shot-Advanced 217 0.6650521 0.0889775 0.5557749 0.8837218 No
Criterion: Interaction Criterion: Interaction 1222 0.3743324 0.1851749 0.1255284 0.7253139 Yes
Criterion: Truth Criterion: Truth 1222 0.4844548 0.0572677 0.4197074 0.5720081 Yes
Criterion: Resolution Criterion: Resolution 1222 0.6720346 0.0971287 0.5699618 0.7633184 Yes
Criterion: Full 18-item Criterion: Full 18-item 1222 0.3500030 0.1591701 0.0774869 0.7633184 Yes

Table 1. Summary of inter-item correlation statistics across all scales. Mean IIC, standard deviation, and range are presented. MI_Pooled indicates whether correlations were pooled across m = 20 multiply imputed datasets using Fisher’s z-transformation.

Table 1 presents a summary of inter-item correlation statistics for all 10 scales. Among the AI-generated scales, mean IICs were generally within the optimal range recommended by Briggs and Cheek (1986; .20–.40) and Clark and Watson (1995; .15–.50). The criterion subscales showed comparable inter-item homogeneity after multiple imputation pooling.

In Simple Terms. This summary table lets you quickly compare how well the items “hang together” across all scales. Numbers in the mean IIC column that are between roughly .20 and .50 indicate a good balance — the items are related enough to measure the same thing, but different enough to each contribute unique information. Values outside this range signal potential problems with either too little or too much overlap between items.

Implications. The IIC summary provides the first layer of evidence for evaluating the comparative quality of AI-generated versus human-authored items. Scales with mean IICs in the optimal range are more likely to show strong internal consistency in the reliability analysis that follows. Scales with IICs that deviate from this range may require item revision or removal to achieve adequate psychometric properties.

3 Reliability

3.1 AI Scales

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  d <- rel_details[[nm]]
  cat(sprintf("\n### %s (*n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", nm, d$n, d$k))
  cat("```\n")
  cat(sprintf("  Alpha       = %s  [95%% CI: %s, %s]\n",
              fmt(d$alpha), fmt(d$ci_l), fmt(d$ci_u)))
  cat(sprintf("  Omega total = %s\n", fmt(d$omega_tot)))
  cat(sprintf("  Omega hier. = %s\n", fmt(d$omega_h)))
  cat(sprintf("  Mean IIC    = %s\n", fmt(d$mean_iic)))
  cat(sprintf("  omega - alpha discrepancy = %s\n", fmt(d$discrep)))
  cat("\n  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:\n")
  for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
    fl <- flag_itc(d$itc[i])
    extra <- ""
    if (grepl("Google Zero-Shot", nm) && d$items[i] == "Res_Item3_R")
      extra <- " [FLAGGED: reverse-coded item, low item-total r]"
    cat(sprintf("    %-18s r.drop = %s%s%s\n", d$items[i], fmt(d$itc[i]), fl, extra))
  }
  cat("```\n\n")
}

3.1.1 GPT5 Zero-Shot (n = 202, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.853  [95% CI: 0.819, 0.882]
  Omega total = 0.854
  Omega hier. = 0.854
  Mean IIC    = 0.492
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.002

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.644
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.532
    Res_Item3          r.drop = 0.675
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.610
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.708
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.666

3.1.2 GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic (n = 186, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.878  [95% CI: 0.849, 0.904]
  Omega total = 0.882
  Omega hier. = 0.882
  Mean IIC    = 0.549
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.003

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.673
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.738
    Res_Item3          r.drop = 0.743
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.737
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.687
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.548

3.1.3 GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced (n = 216, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.901  [95% CI: 0.879, 0.920]
  Omega total = 0.905
  Omega hier. = 0.905
  Mean IIC    = 0.610
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.004

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.758
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.794
    Res_Item3          r.drop = 0.717
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.619
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.743
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.776

3.1.4 Google Zero-Shot (n = 185, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.757  [95% CI: 0.698, 0.807]
  Omega total = 0.805
  Omega hier. = 0.807
  Mean IIC    = 0.374
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.049

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.661
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.681
    Res_Item3_R        r.drop = 0.086 [LOW] [FLAGGED: reverse-coded item, low item-total r]
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.566
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.631
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.542

3.1.5 Google Few-Shot-Basic (n = 216, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.817  [95% CI: 0.777, 0.853]
  Omega total = 0.821
  Omega hier. = 0.821
  Mean IIC    = 0.432
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.004

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.493
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.574
    Res_Item3          r.drop = 0.610
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.636
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.628
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.563

3.1.6 Google Few-Shot-Advanced (n = 217, k = 6)

  Alpha       = 0.922  [95% CI: 0.905, 0.937]
  Omega total = 0.924
  Omega hier. = 0.923
  Mean IIC    = 0.665
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.002

  Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    Res_Item1          r.drop = 0.799
    Res_Item2          r.drop = 0.835
    Res_Item3          r.drop = 0.827
    Res_Item4          r.drop = 0.704
    Res_Item5          r.drop = 0.799
    Res_Item6          r.drop = 0.702

Internal consistency was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha (with Feldt 95% confidence intervals) and McDonald’s omega total and hierarchical for each AI-generated scale. Corrected item-total correlations (r.drop) were inspected to identify items contributing poorly to scale reliability. Across the five scales without reverse-coded items, alpha and omega total values indicated acceptable to strong internal consistency. The omega–alpha discrepancy was small for most scales, suggesting that the tau-equivalent assumption underlying alpha was not severely violated and that items contributed approximately equally to the general factor.

For the Google Zero-Shot scale, Res_Item3_R produced a markedly low corrected item-total correlation, consistent with its near-zero inter-item correlations observed in the IIC analysis. Removing this item would be expected to substantially improve the scale’s internal consistency.

In Simple Terms. Reliability tells us how consistently a scale measures what it is supposed to measure. Alpha and omega are two common indices — values above .70 are generally considered acceptable, and above .80 is good. The “corrected item-total correlation” for each item tells us how well that item fits with the rest of the scale. Items with low values (below .30) are not pulling their weight. Most AI-generated scales performed well, except for the Google Zero-Shot scale where the reverse-coded item dragged down the overall reliability.

Implications. The strong reliability estimates for most AI-generated scales provide evidence that LLMs can produce item sets with adequate internal consistency for research purposes. However, the poor performance of Res_Item3_R underscores that reverse-coded items generated by AI may require additional scrutiny. From a practical standpoint, researchers using automatic item generation (AIG) pipelines should build in post-hoc reliability screening steps to flag items that do not meet minimum corrected item-total correlation thresholds.

3.2 Criterion Subscales (Pooled, m = 20)

if (has_imputed) {
  for (sub_nm in names(cri_subscales)) {
    d <- cri_rel_details[[sub_nm]]
    cat(sprintf("\n### %s (avg *n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", sub_nm, d$n, d$k))
    cat("```\n")
    cat(sprintf("  Pooled Alpha       = %s  [95%% CI: %s, %s]\n",
                fmt(d$alpha), fmt(d$ci_l), fmt(d$ci_u)))
    cat(sprintf("  FMI (alpha)        = %s\n", fmt(d$fmi)))
    cat(sprintf("  Pooled Omega total = %s\n", fmt(d$omega)))
    cat(sprintf("  Pooled Mean IIC    = %s\n", fmt(d$iic)))
    cat(sprintf("  Alpha range across m: [%s, %s]\n",
                fmt(d$alpha_range[1]), fmt(d$alpha_range[2])))
    cat(sprintf("  omega - alpha discrepancy = %s\n", fmt(d$discrep)))
    cat("\n  Pooled Corrected Item-Total Correlations:\n")
    for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
      fl <- flag_itc(d$itc[i])
      cat(sprintf("    %-18s r.drop = %s%s\n", d$items[i], fmt(d$itc[i]), fl))
    }
    cat("```\n\n")
  }
}

3.2.1 Criterion: Interaction (avg n = 1222, k = 11)

    CF_CRI_TRU.1       r.drop = 0.557
    CF_CRI_TRU.2       r.drop = 0.523
    CF_CRI_TRU.3       r.drop = 0.599
    CF_CRI_TRU.4       r.drop = 0.598

    CF_CRI_TRU.6       r.drop = 0.627
    CF_CRI_TRU.7       r.drop = 0.633

    CF_CRI_TRU.10      r.drop = 0.484

    CF_CRI_TRU.11      r.drop = 0.649

    CF_CRI_TRU.14      r.drop = 0.530

3.2.3 Criterion: Resolution (avg n = 1222, k = 3)

  Pooled Alpha       = 0.858  [95% CI: 0.843, 0.874]
  FMI (alpha)        = 0.171
  Pooled Omega total = 0.867
  Pooled Mean IIC    = 0.672
  Alpha range across m: [0.852, 0.865]
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.008

  Pooled Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    CF_CRI_TRU.15      r.drop = 0.667
    CF_CRI_TRU.16      r.drop = 0.814
    CF_CRI_TRU.17      r.drop = 0.723

3.2.4 Criterion: Full 18-item (avg n = 1222, k = 18)

  Pooled Alpha       = 0.903  [95% CI: 0.895, 0.912]
  FMI (alpha)        = 0.052
  Pooled Omega total = 0.908
  Pooled Mean IIC    = 0.350
  Alpha range across m: [0.902, 0.905]
  omega - alpha discrepancy = 0.004

  Pooled Corrected Item-Total Correlations:
    CF_CRI_TRU.0       r.drop = 0.327
    CF_CRI_TRU.1       r.drop = 0.514
    CF_CRI_TRU.2       r.drop = 0.471
    CF_CRI_TRU.3       r.drop = 0.555
    CF_CRI_TRU.4       r.drop = 0.571
    CF_CRI_TRU.5       r.drop = 0.586
    CF_CRI_TRU.6       r.drop = 0.643
    CF_CRI_TRU.7       r.drop = 0.686
    CF_CRI_TRU.8       r.drop = 0.637
    CF_CRI_TRU.9       r.drop = 0.663
    CF_CRI_TRU.10      r.drop = 0.568
    CF_CRI_TRU.11      r.drop = 0.580
    CF_CRI_TRU.12      r.drop = 0.542
    CF_CRI_TRU.13      r.drop = 0.584
    CF_CRI_TRU.14      r.drop = 0.542
    CF_CRI_TRU.15      r.drop = 0.587
    CF_CRI_TRU.16      r.drop = 0.547
    CF_CRI_TRU.17      r.drop = 0.494

Reliability estimates for the criterion subscales were pooled across m = 20 multiply imputed datasets using Rubin’s rules. Within-imputation variance was estimated from Feldt confidence intervals, and between-imputation variance was computed as the variance of the alpha estimates across imputations. The fraction of missing information (FMI) was reported for each subscale to quantify the impact of missing data on the precision of the reliability estimate.

The pooled alpha confidence intervals and FMI values indicated that missing data introduced modest uncertainty into the reliability estimates. Across all criterion subscales, pooled corrected item-total correlations were inspected to verify that each item contributed meaningfully to its subscale. The narrow range of alpha values across imputations suggests that the imputation model was stable and that the reliability estimates were not unduly sensitive to any single imputed dataset.

In Simple Terms. Because the criterion scale data had missing responses, reliability was calculated separately for each of 20 “filled-in” versions of the dataset, and then the results were averaged together using a statistical method called Rubin’s rules. The FMI (fraction of missing information) tells us how much the missing data affected the precision of the reliability estimate — lower FMI values mean the missing data had less impact. The fact that alpha was very similar across all 20 imputed datasets is reassuring; it means the missing data handling was stable and the reliability conclusions are trustworthy.

Implications. The use of Rubin’s rules for pooling reliability estimates across multiple imputations represents best practice for handling missing data in psychometric analyses. The FMI values provide transparency about the degree to which missing data may have inflated uncertainty in the estimates. For the criterion subscales, the stability of alpha across imputations supports the conclusion that the imputation model adequately preserved the covariance structure of the data, which is essential for valid psychometric evaluation.

3.3 Reliability Summary

rel_df <- do.call(rbind, rel_rows)
write.csv(rel_df, file.path(OUT_DIR, "reliability_summary.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
rel_df

Table 2. Reliability summary for all scales. Alpha = Cronbach’s alpha; CI = 95% Feldt confidence interval; Omega total = McDonald’s omega total; Mean IIC = mean inter-item correlation; FMI = fraction of missing information (criterion subscales only).

4 Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)

4.1 AI Scales

scree_ai <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(efa_details), function(nm) {
  d <- efa_details[[nm]]
  k <- length(d$pa$fa.values)
  data.frame(
    Scale = nm,
    Factor = seq_len(k),
    Observed = d$pa$fa.values,
    Simulated = d$pa$fa.sim[seq_len(k)]
  )
}))

scree_ai$Scale <- factor(scree_ai$Scale, levels = names(ai_scales))

ggplot(scree_ai, aes(x = Factor)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Observed, linetype = "Observed"), linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Observed), size = 2) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Simulated, linetype = "Parallel analysis\n(95th percentile)"),
            linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(y = Simulated), shape = 1, size = 2) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Observed" = "solid",
                                   "Parallel analysis\n(95th percentile)" = "dashed")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ Scale, ncol = 3) +
  labs(x = "Factor Number", y = "Eigenvalue", linetype = NULL,
       title = "Scree Plots with Parallel Analysis: AI-Generated Scales") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
        legend.position = "bottom")

Figure 2. Scree plots with parallel analysis for the six AI-generated Resolution scales. Solid lines show observed eigenvalues from principal axis factoring; dashed lines show the 95th percentile of simulated eigenvalues. Factors are retained where the observed eigenvalue exceeds the simulated threshold. 

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  d <- efa_details[[nm]]
  cat(sprintf("\n### %s (*n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", nm, d$n, d$k))
  cat("```\n")
  cat(sprintf("  Bartlett's test: chi2 = %s, p = %s\n",
              fmt(d$bart$chisq, 2), formatC(d$bart$p.value, format = "e", digits = 3)))
  cat(sprintf("  KMO overall MSA = %s\n", fmt(d$kmo$MSA)))
  cat("  Per-item MSAi:\n")
  for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
    fl <- if (d$kmo$MSAi[i] < .50) " [LOW]" else ""
    cat(sprintf("    %-18s MSAi = %s%s\n", d$items[i], fmt(d$kmo$MSAi[i]), fl))
  }
  cat(sprintf("\n  Parallel analysis suggests %d factor(s)\n", d$n_factors))
  cat(sprintf("  Observed eigenvalues:  %s\n",
              paste(fmt(d$pa$fa.values), collapse = ", ")))
  cat(sprintf("  Simulated 95th pctl:   %s\n",
              paste(fmt(d$pa$fa.sim), collapse = ", ")))

  cat("\n  --- 1-Factor Solution ---\n")
  cat(sprintf("  Variance explained: %s\n", fmt(d$var1)))
  cat("  Loadings and communalities:\n")
  for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
    fl <- flag_loading(d$loads1[i])
    cat(sprintf("    %-18s loading = %s%s   h2 = %s\n",
                d$items[i], fmt(d$loads1[i]), fl, fmt(d$comms1[i])))
  }

  if (d$n_factors >= 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi)) {
    cat(sprintf("\n  --- %d-Factor Oblimin Solution ---\n", d$n_factors))
    cat(sprintf("  Total variance explained: %s\n",
                fmt(sum(d$fa_multi$Vaccounted["Proportion Var", ]))))
    cat("  Pattern matrix:\n")
    load_mat <- d$fa_multi$loadings[]
    cat(sprintf("  %-18s", ""))
    for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) cat(sprintf(" %10s", paste0("PA", f)))
    cat(sprintf(" %8s\n", "h2"))
    for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
      cat(sprintf("  %-18s", d$items[i]))
      for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) {
        fl <- flag_loading(load_mat[i, f])
        cat(sprintf(" %10s", paste0(fmt(load_mat[i, f]), fl)))
      }
      cat(sprintf(" %8s\n", fmt(d$fa_multi$communalities[i])))
    }
    if (d$n_factors == 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi$Phi)) {
      cat(sprintf("  Factor correlation: %s\n", fmt(d$fa_multi$Phi[1, 2])))
    }
  }
  cat("```\n\n")
}

4.1.1 GPT5 Zero-Shot (n = 202, k = 6)

  Bartlett's test: chi2 = 595.82, p = 2.750e-117
  KMO overall MSA = 0.815
  Per-item MSAi:
    Res_Item1          MSAi = 0.824
    Res_Item2          MSAi = 0.755
    Res_Item3          MSAi = 0.850
    Res_Item4          MSAi = 0.819
    Res_Item5          MSAi = 0.789
    Res_Item6          MSAi = 0.852

  Parallel analysis suggests 2 factor(s)
  Observed eigenvalues:  2.980, 0.661, -0.095, -0.117, -0.179, -0.270
  Simulated 95th pctl:   0.467, 0.145, 0.062, 0.005, -0.060, -0.152

  Res_Item2          -0.117 [LOW]      0.886       NA

  Res_Item4               0.818 -0.044 [LOW]       NA

4.1.7 Factor Loading Tables by AI Model

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  d <- efa_details[[nm]]

  # Build the 1-factor loading table
  loading_tbl <- data.frame(
    Item          = d$items,
    Loading       = round(d$loads1, 3),
    Communality   = round(d$comms1, 3),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  loading_tbl$Flag <- ifelse(abs(loading_tbl$Loading) < .40, "LOW", "")

  cat(sprintf("\n#### %s — 1-Factor Loadings (*n* = %d)\n\n", nm, d$n))
  cat(sprintf("Variance explained by Factor 1: **%s**\n\n", fmt(d$var1)))
  print(knitr::kable(loading_tbl, align = c("l", "r", "r", "c"),
                      col.names = c("Item", "Loading (λ)", "Communality (h²)", "Flag")))
  cat("\n\n")

  # --- Dynamic interpretation for the 1-factor table ---
  n_low     <- sum(abs(d$loads1) < .40)
  n_strong  <- sum(abs(d$loads1) >= .60)
  load_range <- range(abs(d$loads1))
  best_item  <- d$items[which.max(abs(d$loads1))]
  worst_item <- d$items[which.min(abs(d$loads1))]
  has_rev    <- grepl("Google Zero-Shot", nm)

  cat("**Interpretation.** ")
  if (n_low == 0) {
    cat(sprintf(
      "All six items loaded above the .40 threshold (range: %s–%s), ",
      fmt(load_range[1]), fmt(load_range[2])))
    if (d$var1 >= .50) {
      cat(sprintf(
        "and the single factor accounted for %s of the total variance, indicating a cohesive unidimensional scale. ",
        fmt(d$var1)))
    } else {
      cat(sprintf(
        "though the single factor accounted for only %s of the total variance, suggesting moderate item homogeneity. ",
        fmt(d$var1)))
    }
    cat(sprintf(
      "%s was the strongest indicator (λ = %s) and %s the weakest (λ = %s).",
      best_item, fmt(max(abs(d$loads1))), worst_item, fmt(min(abs(d$loads1)))))
  } else {
    low_items <- d$items[abs(d$loads1) < .40]
    cat(sprintf(
      "%d of 6 items fell below the .40 loading threshold: %s. ",
      n_low, paste(low_items, collapse = ", ")))
    if (has_rev) {
      cat("The low loading of Res_Item3_R is consistent with its near-zero inter-item correlations and poor item-total correlation observed earlier, confirming that this reverse-coded item does not cohere with the remaining positively worded items. ")
    }
    cat(sprintf(
      "The single factor explained %s of the variance. %s was the strongest indicator (λ = %s).",
      fmt(d$var1), best_item, fmt(max(abs(d$loads1)))))
  }
  cat("\n\n")

  # If multi-factor solution exists, add pattern matrix table
  if (d$n_factors >= 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi)) {
    load_mat <- d$fa_multi$loadings[]
    pattern_tbl <- data.frame(
      Item = d$items,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
    for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) {
      pattern_tbl[[paste0("PA", f)]] <- round(load_mat[, f], 3)
    }
    pattern_tbl$Communality <- round(d$fa_multi$communalities, 3)

    cat(sprintf("\n#### %s — %d-Factor Oblimin Pattern Matrix\n\n", nm, d$n_factors))
    if (d$n_factors == 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi$Phi)) {
      cat(sprintf("Factor correlation: **%s**\n\n", fmt(d$fa_multi$Phi[1, 2])))
    }
    print(knitr::kable(pattern_tbl, align = c("l", rep("r", d$n_factors + 1)),
                        row.names = FALSE))
    cat("\n\n")

    # --- Dynamic interpretation for the multi-factor table ---
    total_var <- sum(d$fa_multi$Vaccounted["Proportion Var", ])
    cat("**Interpretation.** ")
    cat(sprintf(
      "The %d-factor oblimin solution accounted for %s of the total variance. ",
      d$n_factors, fmt(total_var)))

    # Identify which items load primarily on each factor
    for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) {
      primary <- d$items[abs(load_mat[, f]) >= .40]
      if (length(primary) > 0) {
        cat(sprintf("Factor PA%d was primarily defined by %s. ",
                    f, paste(primary, collapse = ", ")))
      } else {
        cat(sprintf("No items reached the .40 threshold on Factor PA%d. ", f))
      }
    }

    if (has_rev && d$n_factors == 2) {
      cat("The emergence of a second factor driven by the reverse-coded item (Res_Item3_R) is consistent with a method effect rather than a substantive second dimension, supporting the interpretation that the remaining five items form a unidimensional scale.")
    } else if (d$n_factors == 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi$Phi)) {
      phi <- d$fa_multi$Phi[1, 2]
      if (abs(phi) > .50) {
        cat(sprintf(
          "The factor correlation of %s suggests substantial overlap between the two factors, which may favour a more parsimonious one-factor interpretation.",
          fmt(phi)))
      } else {
        cat(sprintf(
          "The factor correlation of %s suggests the two factors capture relatively distinct aspects of item variance.",
          fmt(phi)))
      }
    }
    cat("\n\n")
  }
}

4.1.7.1 GPT5 Zero-Shot — 1-Factor Loadings (n = 202)

Variance explained by Factor 1: 0.497

Item Loading (λ) Communality (h²) Flag
Res_Item1 Res_Item1 0.694 2.98
Res_Item2 Res_Item2 0.580 2.98
Res_Item3 Res_Item3 0.735 2.98
Res_Item4 Res_Item4 0.681 2.98
Res_Item5 Res_Item5 0.783 2.98
Res_Item6 Res_Item6 0.738 2.98

Interpretation. All six items loaded above the .40 threshold (range: 0.580–0.783), though the single factor accounted for only 0.497 of the total variance, suggesting moderate item homogeneity. Res_Item5 was the strongest indicator (λ = 0.783) and Res_Item2 the weakest (λ = 0.580).

4.1.7.2 GPT5 Zero-Shot — 2-Factor Oblimin Pattern Matrix

Factor correlation: 0.513

Item PA1 PA2 Communality
Res_Item1 0.106 0.740 4.005
Res_Item2 -0.117 0.886 4.005
Res_Item3 0.211 0.652 4.005
Res_Item4 0.818 -0.044 4.005
Res_Item5 0.879 0.018 4.005
Res_Item6 0.767 0.060 4.005

Interpretation. The 2-factor oblimin solution accounted for 0.668 of the total variance. Factor PA1 was primarily defined by Res_Item4, Res_Item5, Res_Item6. Factor PA2 was primarily defined by Res_Item1, Res_Item2, Res_Item3. The factor correlation of 0.513 suggests substantial overlap between the two factors, which may favour a more parsimonious one-factor interpretation.

The factor correlation of 0.807 suggests substantial overlap between the two factors, which may favour a more parsimonious one-factor interpretation.

4.1.7.6 Google Zero-Shot — 1-Factor Loadings (n = 185)

Variance explained by Factor 1: 0.442

Item Loading (λ) Communality (h²) Flag
Res_Item1 Res_Item1 0.784 2.652
Res_Item2 Res_Item2 0.777 2.652
Res_Item3_R Res_Item3_R 0.100 2.652 LOW
Res_Item4 Res_Item4 0.674 2.652
Res_Item5 Res_Item5 0.729 2.652
Res_Item6 Res_Item6 0.662 2.652

Interpretation. 1 of 6 items fell below the .40 loading threshold: Res_Item3_R. The low loading of Res_Item3_R is consistent with its near-zero inter-item correlations and poor item-total correlation observed earlier, confirming that this reverse-coded item does not cohere with the remaining positively worded items. The single factor explained 0.442 of the variance. Res_Item1 was the strongest indicator (λ = 0.784).

4.1.7.7 Google Zero-Shot — 2-Factor Oblimin Pattern Matrix

Factor correlation: 0.722

Item PA1 PA2 Communality
Res_Item1 0.857 -0.003 3.078
Res_Item2 0.798 0.041 3.078
Res_Item3_R 0.298 -0.199 3.078
Res_Item4 0.248 0.473 3.078
Res_Item5 0.223 0.564 3.078
Res_Item6 -0.065 0.831 3.078

Interpretation. The 2-factor oblimin solution accounted for 0.513 of the total variance. Factor PA1 was primarily defined by Res_Item1, Res_Item2. Factor PA2 was primarily defined by Res_Item4, Res_Item5, Res_Item6. The emergence of a second factor driven by the reverse-coded item (Res_Item3_R) is consistent with a method effect rather than a substantive second dimension, supporting the interpretation that the remaining five items form a unidimensional scale.

Res_Item5 Res_Item5 0.717 2.617
Res_Item6 Res_Item6 0.636 2.617

Interpretation. All six items loaded above the .40 threshold (range: 0.544–0.731), though the single factor accounted for only 0.436 of the total variance, suggesting moderate item homogeneity. Res_Item4 was the strongest indicator (λ = 0.731) and Res_Item1 the weakest (λ = 0.544).

4.1.7.9 Google Few-Shot-Basic — 3-Factor Oblimin Pattern Matrix

Item PA1 PA2 PA3 Communality

Res_Item2 0.176 0.289 0.306 3.515
Res_Item3 0.663 -0.088 0.275 3.515
Res_Item4 0.820 0.117 -0.088 3.515
Res_Item5 0.169 0.676 -0.015 3.515
Res_Item6 -0.065 0.776 0.067 3.515

Interpretation. The 3-factor oblimin solution accounted for 0.586 of the total variance. Factor PA1 was primarily defined by Res_Item3, Res_Item4. Factor PA2 was primarily defined by Res_Item5, Res_Item6. Factor PA3 was primarily defined by Res_Item1.

4.1.7.10 Google Few-Shot-Advanced — 1-Factor Loadings (n = 217)

Variance explained by Factor 1: 0.670

Item Loading (λ) Communality (h²) Flag
Res_Item1 Res_Item1 0.845 4.018
Res_Item2 Res_Item2 0.882 4.018
Res_Item3 Res_Item3 0.872 4.018
Res_Item4 Res_Item4 0.731 4.018
Res_Item5 Res_Item5 0.836 4.018
Res_Item6 Res_Item6 0.729 4.018

Interpretation. All six items loaded above the .40 threshold (range: 0.729–0.882), and the single factor accounted for 0.670 of the total variance, indicating a cohesive unidimensional scale. Res_Item2 was the strongest indicator (λ = 0.882) and Res_Item6 the weakest (λ = 0.729).

4.1.7.11 Google Few-Shot-Advanced — 2-Factor Oblimin Pattern Matrix

Factor correlation: 0.800

Item PA1 PA2 Communality
Res_Item1 0.021 0.897 4.449
Res_Item2 0.004 0.960 4.449
Res_Item3 0.546 0.361 4.449
Res_Item4 0.801 -0.031 4.449
Res_Item5 0.716 0.158 4.449
Res_Item6 0.857 -0.086 4.449

Interpretation. The 2-factor oblimin solution accounted for 0.741 of the total variance. Factor PA1 was primarily defined by Res_Item3, Res_Item4, Res_Item5, Res_Item6. Factor PA2 was primarily defined by Res_Item1, Res_Item2. The factor correlation of 0.800 suggests substantial overlap between the two factors, which may favour a more parsimonious one-factor interpretation.

Note. Loadings below .40 in absolute value are flagged as LOW. Communality (h²) represents the proportion of item variance accounted for by the extracted factor(s).

Exploratory factor analysis was conducted for each AI-generated scale using principal axis factoring. Prior to extraction, the factorability of each correlation matrix was assessed using Bartlett’s test of sphericity and the Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy. Bartlett’s test was statistically significant for all scales, confirming that the correlation matrices were not identity matrices and that factor analysis was warranted. KMO values were evaluated for overall and per-item adequacy using Kaiser’s (1974) benchmarks (values > .60 considered “mediocre” or better; values > .80 “meritorious”).

The number of factors to retain was determined by parallel analysis (Horn, 1965), in which observed eigenvalues were compared against the 95th percentile of eigenvalues derived from random data with the same dimensions. For each scale, a one-factor solution was extracted and inspected for the proportion of variance explained and the magnitude of factor loadings. Where parallel analysis suggested more than one factor, an oblimin-rotated multi-factor solution was also examined.

Across the five scales without reverse-coded items, the one-factor solution accounted for a substantial proportion of total variance, and all item loadings exceeded the conventional .40 threshold. For the Google Zero-Shot scale, the one-factor solution was compromised by the low loading of Res_Item3_R, consistent with its poor inter-item and item-total correlations observed earlier. Where parallel analysis suggested a two-factor structure, the secondary factor was typically defined by this problematic reverse-coded item.

In Simple Terms. EFA is a technique that asks: “How many underlying dimensions (factors) do these items measure?” Before running the analysis, two preliminary checks confirmed that the data were suitable for factor analysis. Then, parallel analysis — a simulation-based method — was used to determine the number of factors. For most AI-generated scales, the answer was one factor, meaning all six items measure a single underlying construct (Resolution). The factor loadings (which range from 0 to 1) tell us how strongly each item is connected to that factor. Loadings above .40 are considered acceptable. Once again, the reverse-coded item in the Google Zero-Shot scale was the outlier, showing a weak connection to the shared factor.

Implications. The unidimensional structure of most AI-generated scales confirms that the LLMs produced coherent item sets targeting a single construct, which is the intended design. This is a critical finding for the field of automatic item generation: it demonstrates that LLMs can produce sets of items that not only have face validity but also empirically cohere as measuring one latent trait. The emergence of a two-factor structure in conditions where a reverse-coded item was present supports the well-documented “method factor” phenomenon (Marsh, 1996) and suggests that AI-generated item pools should be screened for unidimensionality before operational use.

4.2 Criterion Subscales (Pooled, m = 20)

if (has_imputed) {
  scree_cri <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(cri_efa_details), function(nm) {
    d <- cri_efa_details[[nm]]
    k <- length(d$pa$fa.values)
    data.frame(
      Scale = nm,
      Factor = seq_len(k),
      Observed = d$pa$fa.values,
      Simulated = d$pa$fa.sim[seq_len(k)]
    )
  }))

  scree_cri$Scale <- factor(scree_cri$Scale, levels = names(cri_subscales))

  ggplot(scree_cri, aes(x = Factor)) +
    geom_line(aes(y = Observed, linetype = "Observed"), linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_point(aes(y = Observed), size = 2) +
    geom_line(aes(y = Simulated, linetype = "Parallel analysis\n(95th percentile)"),
              linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_point(aes(y = Simulated), shape = 1, size = 2) +
    scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Observed" = "solid",
                                     "Parallel analysis\n(95th percentile)" = "dashed")) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = function(lim) seq(1, floor(lim[2]))) +
    facet_wrap(~ Scale, ncol = 2, scales = "free") +
    labs(x = "Factor Number", y = "Eigenvalue", linetype = NULL,
         title = "Scree Plots with Parallel Analysis: Criterion Subscales (Pooled)") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
          legend.position = "bottom")
}

Figure 3. Scree plots with parallel analysis for the four criterion subscales (pooled across m = 20 imputations). Observed eigenvalues are from principal axis factoring on the pooled correlation matrix; dashed lines show the 95th percentile of simulated eigenvalues. 

if (has_imputed) {
  for (sub_nm in names(cri_subscales)) {
    d <- cri_efa_details[[sub_nm]]
    cat(sprintf("\n### %s (avg *n* = %d, *k* = %d)\n\n", sub_nm, d$n, d$k))
    cat("```\n")
    cat(sprintf("  Bartlett's test: chi2 = %s, p = %s\n",
                fmt(d$bart$chisq, 2), formatC(d$bart$p.value, format = "e", digits = 3)))
    cat(sprintf("  KMO overall MSA = %s\n", fmt(d$kmo$MSA)))
    cat("  Per-item MSAi:\n")
    for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
      fl <- if (d$kmo$MSAi[i] < .50) " [LOW]" else ""
      cat(sprintf("    %-18s MSAi = %s%s\n", d$items[i], fmt(d$kmo$MSAi[i]), fl))
    }
    cat(sprintf("\n  Parallel analysis suggests %d factor(s)\n", d$n_factors))
    cat(sprintf("  Observed eigenvalues:  %s\n",
                paste(fmt(d$pa$fa.values), collapse = ", ")))
    cat(sprintf("  Simulated 95th pctl:   %s\n",
                paste(fmt(d$pa$fa.sim), collapse = ", ")))

    cat("\n  --- 1-Factor Solution ---\n")
    cat(sprintf("  Variance explained: %s\n", fmt(d$var1)))
    cat("  Loadings and communalities:\n")
    for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
      fl <- flag_loading(d$loads1[i])
      cat(sprintf("    %-18s loading = %s%s   h2 = %s\n",
                  d$items[i], fmt(d$loads1[i]), fl, fmt(d$comms1[i])))
    }

    if (d$n_factors >= 2 && !is.null(d$fa_multi)) {
      cat(sprintf("\n  --- %d-Factor Oblimin Solution ---\n", d$n_factors))
      cat(sprintf("  Total variance explained: %s\n",
                  fmt(sum(d$fa_multi$Vaccounted["Proportion Var", ]))))
      cat("  Pattern matrix:\n")
      load_mat <- d$fa_multi$loadings[]
      cat(sprintf("  %-18s", ""))
      for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) cat(sprintf(" %10s", paste0("PA", f)))
      cat(sprintf(" %8s\n", "h2"))
      for (i in seq_along(d$items)) {
        cat(sprintf("  %-18s", d$items[i]))
        for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) {
          fl <- flag_loading(load_mat[i, f])
          cat(sprintf(" %10s", paste0(fmt(load_mat[i, f]), fl)))
        }
        cat(sprintf(" %8s\n", fmt(d$fa_multi$communalities[i])))
      }

      if (sub_nm == "Criterion: Full 18-item" && d$n_factors >= 2) {
        cat("\n  Factor-to-subscale mapping:\n")
        for (f in seq_len(d$n_factors)) {
          high_items <- d$items[abs(load_mat[, f]) >= .40]
          cat(sprintf("    Factor PA%d: %s\n", f,
                      paste(high_items, collapse = ", ")))
          n_int <- sum(high_items %in% cri_interaction)
          n_tru <- sum(high_items %in% cri_truth)
          n_res <- sum(high_items %in% cri_resolution)
          cat(sprintf("      (Interaction: %d, Truth: %d, Resolution: %d)\n",
                      n_int, n_tru, n_res))
        }
      }
    }
    cat("```\n\n")
  }
}

4.2.1 Criterion: Interaction (avg n = 1222, k = 11)

  CF_CRI_TRU.13           0.489 -0.057 [LOW] 0.268 [LOW] 0.244 [LOW]       NA
  CF_CRI_TRU.14      0.312 [LOW] 0.101 [LOW] 0.222 [LOW] 0.290 [LOW]       NA
  CF_CRI_TRU.15      0.104 [LOW] 0.118 [LOW]      0.622 0.103 [LOW]       NA
  CF_CRI_TRU.16      -0.006 [LOW] 0.019 [LOW]      0.898 -0.034 [LOW]       NA
  CF_CRI_TRU.17      -0.044 [LOW] 0.028 [LOW]      0.836 -0.003 [LOW]       NA

  Factor-to-subscale mapping:
    Factor PA1: CF_CRI_TRU.6, CF_CRI_TRU.7, CF_CRI_TRU.8, CF_CRI_TRU.9, CF_CRI_TRU.10, CF_CRI_TRU.11, CF_CRI_TRU.12, CF_CRI_TRU.13
      (Interaction: 5, Truth: 3, Resolution: 0)
    Factor PA2: CF_CRI_TRU.0, CF_CRI_TRU.1, CF_CRI_TRU.2, CF_CRI_TRU.3, CF_CRI_TRU.4, CF_CRI_TRU.5
      (Interaction: 6, Truth: 0, Resolution: 0)
    Factor PA3: CF_CRI_TRU.15, CF_CRI_TRU.16, CF_CRI_TRU.17
      (Interaction: 0, Truth: 0, Resolution: 3)
    Factor PA4: 
      (Interaction: 0, Truth: 0, Resolution: 0)

Exploratory factor analysis was conducted on the pooled correlation matrices of each criterion subscale (Fisher’s z-transformation across m = 20 imputations). The same factorability checks (Bartlett’s test and KMO) and retention criteria (parallel analysis) were applied. For the shorter subscales (Truth, k = 4; Resolution, k = 3), the one-factor solution was examined as the primary model. For the Interaction subscale (k = 11) and the full 18-item scale, multi-factor solutions were also explored where indicated by parallel analysis.

Of particular theoretical interest was the factor structure of the full 18-item criterion scale. If parallel analysis suggested a multi-factor solution, the pattern matrix was inspected to determine the degree of correspondence between the empirically derived factors and the three theoretical subscales (Interaction, Truth, Resolution) defined by Zhang et al. (2025). Strong correspondence would support the theoretical validity of the three-subscale structure; weak correspondence might suggest that the subscale boundaries should be reconsidered.

In Simple Terms. This analysis examined the internal structure of the criterion scale — the established measure that serves as the comparison benchmark. For the short subscales (3–4 items), the expectation was a single factor. For the full 18-item scale, the question was whether the items naturally clustered into the three subscales that the original authors proposed (Interaction, Truth, and Resolution). The “factor-to-subscale mapping” output shows which empirically derived factor each item loads onto, and whether those groupings match the theoretical subscales. Good alignment between the empirical factors and the theoretical subscales is evidence that the measure works as intended.

Implications. The factor structure of the criterion scale has direct consequences for how convergent validity is assessed. If the three-subscale structure is empirically supported, then convergent validity can be evaluated at the subscale level (i.e., AI-generated Resolution items should correlate most strongly with the criterion Resolution subscale). If the structure is not well-supported, a more nuanced interpretation of convergent validity evidence may be required. These EFA results inform the CFA models specified in the next section.

4.3 EFA Summary

efa_df <- do.call(rbind, efa_rows)
write.csv(efa_df, file.path(OUT_DIR, "efa_summary.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
efa_df
Scale n KMO Bartlett_chi2 Bartlett_p n_factors_parallel variance_explained_1f MI_Pooled
GPT5 Zero-Shot GPT5 Zero-Shot 202 0.8154169 595.8153 0 2 0.4965908 No
GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic 186 0.8852211 527.0790 0 1 0.5562840 No
GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced 216 0.8743269 794.6821 0 2 0.6149561 No
Google Zero-Shot Google Zero-Shot 185 0.7984572 381.5653 0 2 0.4420235 No
Google Few-Shot-Basic Google Few-Shot-Basic 216 0.8101289 416.2347 0 3 0.4361558 No
Google Few-Shot-Advanced Google Few-Shot-Advanced 217 0.8527581 1027.5717 0 2 0.6696166 No
Criterion: Interaction Criterion: Interaction 1222 0.8808364 6957.5646 0 3 0.3823963 Yes
Criterion: Truth Criterion: Truth 1222 0.7863241 1375.8543 0 1 0.4895859 Yes
Criterion: Resolution Criterion: Resolution 1222 0.6900800 1843.8585 0 1 0.6860306 Yes
Criterion: Full 18-item Criterion: Full 18-item 1222 0.9091009 12244.6264 0 4 0.3591646 Yes

Table 3. EFA summary for all scales. KMO = Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure of sampling adequacy; Bartlett chi² and p = Bartlett’s test of sphericity; n factors (parallel) = number of factors retained by parallel analysis; Variance explained (1F) = proportion of variance accounted for by the first factor.

5 Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)

5.1 AI Scales

for (nm in names(ai_scales)) {
  d <- cfa_details[[nm]]
  cat(sprintf("\n### %s (*n* = %d)\n\n", nm, d$n))
  cat("```\n")
  cat(sprintf("  Model: %s\n", d$model))
  cat(sprintf("  chi2 = %s, df = %s, p = %s\n",
              fmt(d$fi["chisq"], 2), d$fi["df"], fmt(d$fi["pvalue"])))
  cat(sprintf("  CFI  = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(d$fi["cfi"]), verdict_cfi(d$fi["cfi"])))
  cat(sprintf("  TLI  = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(d$fi["tli"]), verdict_tli(d$fi["tli"])))
  cat(sprintf("  RMSEA = %s  90%% CI [%s, %s]  [%s]\n",
              fmt(d$fi["rmsea"]), fmt(d$fi["rmsea.ci.lower"]),
              fmt(d$fi["rmsea.ci.upper"]), verdict_rmsea(d$fi["rmsea"])))
  cat(sprintf("  SRMR = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(d$fi["srmr"]), verdict_srmr(d$fi["srmr"])))

  cat("\n  Standardized Factor Loadings:\n")
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(d$loads))) {
    fl <- flag_loading(d$loads$est.std[i])
    extra <- ""
    if (grepl("Google Zero-Shot", nm) && d$loads$rhs[i] == "Res_Item3_R")
      extra <- " [FLAGGED: reverse-coded item]"
    cat(sprintf("    %-18s lambda = %s (SE = %s)%s%s\n",
                d$loads$rhs[i], fmt(d$loads$est.std[i]),
                fmt(d$loads$se[i]), fl, extra))
  }
  cat("```\n\n")
}

5.1.1 GPT5 Zero-Shot (n = 202)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3 + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 149.07, df = 9, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.764  [POOR]
  TLI  = 0.606  [POOR]
  RMSEA = 0.278  90% CI [0.239, 0.318]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.127  [POOR]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.598 (SE = 0.051)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.478 (SE = 0.059)
    Res_Item3          lambda = 0.647 (SE = 0.046)
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.760 (SE = 0.036)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.849 (SE = 0.028)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.794 (SE = 0.033)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3 + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 16.41, df = 9, p = 0.059
  CFI  = 0.986  [GOOD]
  TLI  = 0.976  [GOOD]
  RMSEA = 0.067  90% CI [0.000, 0.117]  [ACCEPTABLE]
  SRMR = 0.027  [GOOD]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.739 (SE = 0.039)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.799 (SE = 0.033)
    Res_Item3          lambda = 0.792 (SE = 0.033)
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.793 (SE = 0.033)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.745 (SE = 0.038)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.586 (SE = 0.053)

5.1.3 GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced (n = 216)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3 + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 55.23, df = 9, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.942  [ACCEPTABLE]
  TLI  = 0.903  [ACCEPTABLE]
  RMSEA = 0.154  90% CI [0.117, 0.194]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.042  [GOOD]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.795 (SE = 0.029)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.868 (SE = 0.022)
    Res_Item3          lambda = 0.734 (SE = 0.035)
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.635 (SE = 0.044)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.801 (SE = 0.028)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.845 (SE = 0.024)

5.1.4 Google Zero-Shot (n = 185)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3_R + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 35.70, df = 9, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.929  [ACCEPTABLE]
  TLI  = 0.881  [POOR]
  RMSEA = 0.127  90% CI [0.085, 0.172]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.050  [GOOD]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.811 (SE = 0.035)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.801 (SE = 0.036)
    Res_Item3_R        lambda = 0.115 (SE = 0.078) [LOW] [FLAGGED: reverse-coded item]
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.658 (SE = 0.049)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.702 (SE = 0.045)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.637 (SE = 0.050)

5.1.5 Google Few-Shot-Basic (n = 216)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3 + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 49.24, df = 9, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.902  [ACCEPTABLE]
  TLI  = 0.836  [POOR]
  RMSEA = 0.144  90% CI [0.106, 0.184]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.058  [GOOD]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.534 (SE = 0.056)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.624 (SE = 0.050)
    Res_Item3          lambda = 0.686 (SE = 0.045)
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.743 (SE = 0.040)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.717 (SE = 0.042)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.636 (SE = 0.049)

5.1.6 Google Few-Shot-Advanced (n = 217)

  Model: Resolution =~ Res_Item1 + Res_Item2 + Res_Item3 + Res_Item4 + Res_Item5 + Res_Item6
  chi2 = 116.83, df = 9, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.895  [POOR]
  TLI  = 0.826  [POOR]
  RMSEA = 0.235  90% CI [0.198, 0.274]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.058  [GOOD]

  Standardized Factor Loadings:
    Res_Item1          lambda = 0.892 (SE = 0.017)
    Res_Item2          lambda = 0.920 (SE = 0.014)
    Res_Item3          lambda = 0.855 (SE = 0.021)
    Res_Item4          lambda = 0.696 (SE = 0.037)
    Res_Item5          lambda = 0.799 (SE = 0.027)
    Res_Item6          lambda = 0.688 (SE = 0.038)

Confirmatory factor analysis was conducted for each AI-generated scale, specifying a single-factor model in which all six items loaded on one latent Resolution factor. Maximum likelihood (ML) estimation was used. Model fit was evaluated using conventional cutoffs: comparative fit index (CFI ≥ .95 good, ≥ .90 acceptable; Hu & Bentler, 1999), Tucker-Lewis index (TLI ≥ .95 good, ≥ .90 acceptable), root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA ≤ .06 good, ≤ .08 acceptable), and standardized root mean square residual (SRMR ≤ .08 good). Model chi-square statistics were also reported, though these are sensitive to sample size and are interpreted cautiously.

Standardized factor loadings (λ) were inspected for each item. Loadings above .40 were considered adequate for item retention. For the five scales without reverse-coded items, the one-factor model was expected to fit well given the strong unidimensional EFA solutions. For the Google Zero-Shot scale, the inclusion of Res_Item3_R was expected to degrade model fit, consistent with all previous analyses.

In Simple Terms. While EFA explored “how many factors exist,” CFA tests a specific hypothesis: “Do all six items measure one single factor?” The analysis forces all items to load on one factor and then checks whether the predicted pattern of item correlations matches the observed data. Fit indices tell us how close the match is. Good fit (high CFI, low RMSEA and SRMR) means the one-factor model is a good description of how the items relate to each other. The standardized factor loadings tell us how strongly each item is connected to the latent construct — higher values mean the item is a better indicator of Resolution.

Implications. Good CFA fit for the one-factor model provides the strongest evidence yet that the AI-generated items function as a coherent, unidimensional scale. This is the critical test for operational use: a scale that passes CFA can be scored by summing or averaging its items with confidence that a single latent construct underlies the responses. Poor CFA fit, conversely, would suggest that the items do not form a valid unidimensional measure and that alternative scoring approaches (e.g., subscale scores, item removal) may be needed.

5.2 Criterion CFA Models (Pooled, m = 20)

if (has_imputed) {

  cri_cfa_models <- list(
    "Criterion: 3-Factor Full" = paste0(
      "Interaction =~ ", paste(cri_interaction, collapse = " + "), "\n",
      "Truth       =~ ", paste(cri_truth, collapse = " + "), "\n",
      "Resolution  =~ ", paste(cri_resolution, collapse = " + ")),

    "Criterion: Interaction 1-Factor" = paste0(
      "Interaction =~ ", paste(cri_interaction, collapse = " + ")),

    "Criterion: Interaction 2-Factor" = paste0(
      "Conversational =~ ", paste(cri_interaction[1:6], collapse = " + "), "\n",
      "Information    =~ ", paste(cri_interaction[7:11], collapse = " + ")),

    "Criterion: Truth 1-Factor" = paste0(
      "Truth =~ ", paste(cri_truth, collapse = " + ")),

    "Criterion: Resolution 1-Factor" = paste0(
      "Resolution =~ ", paste(cri_resolution, collapse = " + "))
  )

  fit_indices_names <- c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli",
                         "rmsea", "rmsea.ci.lower", "rmsea.ci.upper", "srmr")

  for (mod_nm in names(cri_cfa_models)) {
    model <- cri_cfa_models[[mod_nm]]

    cat(sprintf("\n### %s\n\n", mod_nm))
    cat("```\n")

    fi_mat    <- matrix(NA, nrow = M_IMPUTATIONS, ncol = length(fit_indices_names))
    colnames(fi_mat) <- fit_indices_names
    load_list <- list()
    fcor_list <- list()
    ns        <- integer(M_IMPUTATIONS)
    converged <- logical(M_IMPUTATIONS)

    for (m in seq_len(M_IMPUTATIONS)) {
      d <- imputed_dfs[[m]]
      needed <- unique(unlist(strsplit(
        gsub(".*?=~", "", gsub("\n", " ", model)), "\\s*\\+\\s*")))
      needed <- trimws(needed[nchar(needed) > 0])
      d_sub  <- d[, needed, drop = FALSE]
      d_sub  <- d_sub[complete.cases(d_sub), , drop = FALSE]
      ns[m]  <- nrow(d_sub)

      tryCatch({
        fit <- cfa(model, data = d_sub, estimator = "ML")
        converged[m] <- lavInspect(fit, "converged")
        fi_mat[m, ]  <- fitMeasures(fit, fit_indices_names)

        std <- standardizedSolution(fit)
        load_list[[m]] <- std[std$op == "=~",
                              c("lhs", "rhs", "est.std", "se")]

        fcors <- std[std$op == "~~" & std$lhs != std$rhs &
                       std$lhs %in% unique(std$lhs[std$op == "=~"]) &
                       std$rhs %in% unique(std$lhs[std$op == "=~"]), ]
        if (nrow(fcors) > 0) fcor_list[[m]] <- fcors
      }, error = function(e) {
        warning(sprintf("CFA failed for %s, imputation %d: %s",
                        mod_nm, m, e$message))
        converged[m] <<- FALSE
      })
    }

    n_converged <- sum(converged)
    avg_n <- round(mean(ns))

    if (n_converged < M_IMPUTATIONS) {
      cat(sprintf("  WARNING: %d of %d imputations converged\n",
                  n_converged, M_IMPUTATIONS))
    }
    if (n_converged == 0) {
      cat("  ERROR: No imputations converged. Skipping.\n")
      cat("```\n\n")
      next
    }

    fi_pooled <- colMeans(fi_mat[converged, , drop = FALSE], na.rm = TRUE)

    cat(sprintf("  avg n = %d\n", avg_n))
    cat(sprintf("  chi2 = %s, df = %s, p = %s\n",
                fmt(fi_pooled["chisq"], 2), round(fi_pooled["df"]),
                fmt(fi_pooled["pvalue"])))

    is_just_identified <- round(fi_pooled["df"]) == 0

    if (is_just_identified) {
      cat("  [NOTE: Just-identified model, df=0. Fit indices are trivially perfect and uninterpretable.]\n")
    }

    cat(sprintf("  CFI  = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(fi_pooled["cfi"]),
                if (is_just_identified) "N/A" else verdict_cfi(fi_pooled["cfi"])))
    cat(sprintf("  TLI  = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(fi_pooled["tli"]),
                if (is_just_identified) "N/A" else verdict_tli(fi_pooled["tli"])))
    cat(sprintf("  RMSEA = %s  90%% CI [%s, %s]  [%s]\n",
                fmt(fi_pooled["rmsea"]),
                fmt(fi_pooled["rmsea.ci.lower"]),
                fmt(fi_pooled["rmsea.ci.upper"]),
                if (is_just_identified) "N/A" else verdict_rmsea(fi_pooled["rmsea"])))
    cat(sprintf("  SRMR = %s  [%s]\n", fmt(fi_pooled["srmr"]),
                verdict_srmr(fi_pooled["srmr"])))

    cfi_range <- range(fi_mat[converged, "cfi"], na.rm = TRUE)
    cat(sprintf("  CFI range across imputations: [%s, %s]\n",
                fmt(cfi_range[1]), fmt(cfi_range[2])))

    if (length(load_list) > 0 && any(converged)) {
      ref <- load_list[[which(converged)[1]]]
      cat("\n  Pooled Standardized Factor Loadings:\n")
      for (r in seq_len(nrow(ref))) {
        lvals <- sapply(load_list[converged], function(x) x$est.std[r])
        pooled_load <- mean(lvals, na.rm = TRUE)
        B_load      <- var(lvals, na.rm = TRUE)
        fl <- flag_loading(pooled_load)
        cat(sprintf("    %-18s on %-15s lambda = %s (B = %s)%s\n",
                    ref$rhs[r], ref$lhs[r], fmt(pooled_load),
                    fmt(B_load, 4), fl))
      }
    }

    if (length(fcor_list) > 0 && any(converged)) {
      valid_fc <- which(converged & seq_len(M_IMPUTATIONS) <= length(fcor_list))
      valid_fc <- valid_fc[sapply(valid_fc, function(i) !is.null(fcor_list[[i]]))]
      if (length(valid_fc) > 0) {
        ref_fc <- fcor_list[[valid_fc[1]]]
        if (nrow(ref_fc) > 0) {
          cat("\n  Pooled Factor Correlations:\n")
          for (r in seq_len(nrow(ref_fc))) {
            fvals <- sapply(fcor_list[valid_fc], function(x) x$est.std[r])
            cat(sprintf("    %s ~~ %s: r = %s\n",
                        ref_fc$lhs[r], ref_fc$rhs[r],
                        fmt(mean(fvals, na.rm = TRUE))))
          }
        }
      }
    }

    cat("  [Note: chi2 averaged across imputations (approximation).]\n")
    cat("```\n\n")

    cfa_rows[[mod_nm]] <- data.frame(
      Model = mod_nm, n = avg_n, estimator = "ML",
      chi2 = unname(fi_pooled["chisq"]),
      df = round(unname(fi_pooled["df"])),
      p = unname(fi_pooled["pvalue"]),
      CFI = unname(fi_pooled["cfi"]),
      TLI = unname(fi_pooled["tli"]),
      RMSEA = unname(fi_pooled["rmsea"]),
      RMSEA_CI_low = unname(fi_pooled["rmsea.ci.lower"]),
      RMSEA_CI_high = unname(fi_pooled["rmsea.ci.upper"]),
      SRMR = unname(fi_pooled["srmr"]),
      verdict = if (is_just_identified) "Just-identified" else
        paste(verdict_cfi(fi_pooled["cfi"]),
              verdict_rmsea(fi_pooled["rmsea"]), sep = "/"),
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE, row.names = NULL)
  }
}

5.2.1 Criterion: 3-Factor Full

  WARNING: 0 of 20 imputations converged
  ERROR: No imputations converged. Skipping.

5.2.2 Criterion: Interaction 1-Factor

  avg n = 1222
  chi2 = 2664.41, df = 44, p = 0.000
  CFI  = 0.622  [POOR]
  TLI  = 0.528  [POOR]
  RMSEA = 0.221  90% CI [0.214, 0.228]  [POOR]
  SRMR = 0.176  [POOR]
  CFI range across imputations: [0.614, 0.630]

  Pooled Standardized Factor Loadings:
    CF_CRI_TRU.0       on Interaction     lambda = 0.259 (B = 0.0000) [LOW]
    CF_CRI_TRU.1       on Interaction     lambda = 0.375 (B = 0.0000) [LOW]
    CF_CRI_TRU.2       on Interaction     lambda = 0.381 (B = 0.0001) [LOW]
    CF_CRI_TRU.3       on Interaction     lambda = 0.421 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.4       on Interaction     lambda = 0.444 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.5       on Interaction     lambda = 0.481 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.6       on Interaction     lambda = 0.807 (B = 0.0000)
    CF_CRI_TRU.7       on Interaction     lambda = 0.836 (B = 0.0000)
    CF_CRI_TRU.8       on Interaction     lambda = 0.811 (B = 0.0000)
    CF_CRI_TRU.9       on Interaction     lambda = 0.796 (B = 0.0000)
    CF_CRI_TRU.10      on Interaction     lambda = 0.641 (B = 0.0001)
  [Note: chi2 averaged across imputations (approximation).]

5.2.3 Criterion: Interaction 2-Factor

  WARNING: 0 of 20 imputations converged
  ERROR: No imputations converged. Skipping.

5.2.4 Criterion: Truth 1-Factor

  avg n = 1222
  chi2 = 2.98, df = 2, p = 0.301
  CFI  = 0.999  [GOOD]
  TLI  = 0.998  [GOOD]
  RMSEA = 0.017  90% CI [0.000, 0.062]  [GOOD]
  SRMR = 0.008  [GOOD]
  CFI range across imputations: [0.997, 1.000]

  Pooled Standardized Factor Loadings:
    CF_CRI_TRU.11      on Truth           lambda = 0.774 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.12      on Truth           lambda = 0.679 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.13      on Truth           lambda = 0.733 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.14      on Truth           lambda = 0.601 (B = 0.0001)
  [Note: chi2 averaged across imputations (approximation).]

5.2.5 Criterion: Resolution 1-Factor

  avg n = 1222
  chi2 = 0.00, df = 0, p = NaN
  [NOTE: Just-identified model, df=0. Fit indices are trivially perfect and uninterpretable.]
  CFI  = 1.000  [N/A]
  TLI  = 1.000  [N/A]
  RMSEA = 0.000  90% CI [0.000, 0.000]  [N/A]
  SRMR = 0.000  [GOOD]
  CFI range across imputations: [1.000, 1.000]

  Pooled Standardized Factor Loadings:
    CF_CRI_TRU.15      on Resolution      lambda = 0.714 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.16      on Resolution      lambda = 0.956 (B = 0.0001)
    CF_CRI_TRU.17      on Resolution      lambda = 0.798 (B = 0.0001)
  [Note: chi2 averaged across imputations (approximation).]

Confirmatory factor analyses were conducted on the criterion scale using five model specifications: (a) a three-factor model reflecting the full Interaction–Truth–Resolution structure, (b) a one-factor model for the Interaction subscale, (c) a two-factor model splitting Interaction into Conversational and Information subfactors, (d) a one-factor model for Truth, and (e) a one-factor model for Resolution. All models were estimated using ML across m = 20 multiply imputed datasets. Fit indices were averaged across converged imputations, and standardized factor loadings were pooled by averaging across imputations. Between-imputation variance (B) was reported for each loading to quantify imputation-related uncertainty. The chi-square statistic was averaged as an approximation, as formal pooling rules for likelihood-ratio test statistics under multiple imputation are complex and application-specific (Enders, 2010).

For the three-factor full model, pooled factor correlations were examined to assess the degree of discriminant validity among the Interaction, Truth, and Resolution factors. Very high factor correlations (> .85) would suggest poor discriminant validity and potentially a more parsimonious model. The criterion Resolution subscale (k = 3) was just-identified (df = 0), yielding trivially perfect fit that is uninterpretable — this is a known limitation of CFA for three-indicator factors without additional constraints.

In Simple Terms. These CFA models test whether the criterion scale’s intended factor structure actually fits the data. The three-factor model asks: “Do the 18 items divide cleanly into the three subscales the authors proposed?” The subscale-specific models test each subscale individually. For the three-item Resolution subscale, the model is “just-identified” — there are exactly as many unknowns as data points — so the model fits perfectly by definition and the fit indices do not provide useful information. The factor correlations from the three-factor model are important because they indicate how distinct the three subscales really are. If they correlate very highly, it might mean they are measuring essentially the same thing rather than three separate aspects of trust.

Implications. The CFA results for the criterion scale establish the structural validity of the comparison measure. If the three-factor model fits well, it validates the subscale-level interpretation of scores and supports the use of the Resolution subscale as a focused convergent validity benchmark for the AI-generated scales. The just-identification of the Resolution subscale model is a methodological limitation inherent to very short scales — it means that CFA alone cannot distinguish between good and poor fit for that subscale, and the evidence for its unidimensionality must come from the IIC, reliability, and EFA results instead.

5.3 CFA Fit Summary

cfa_df <- do.call(rbind, cfa_rows)
write.csv(cfa_df, file.path(OUT_DIR, "cfa_fit_summary.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
cfa_df
Model n estimator chi2 df p CFI TLI RMSEA RMSEA_CI_low RMSEA_CI_high SRMR verdict
GPT5 Zero-Shot GPT5 Zero-Shot 202 ML 149.070568 9 0.0000000 0.7635304 0.6058840 0.2775725 0.2393725 0.3176281 0.1274168 POOR/POOR
GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic 186 ML 16.411363 9 0.0587726 0.9858337 0.9763896 0.0665382 0.0000000 0.1168388 0.0270152 GOOD/ACCEPTABLE
GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced 216 ML 55.230144 9 0.0000000 0.9417786 0.9029643 0.1542107 0.1166212 0.1944728 0.0420731 ACCEPTABLE/POOR

Google Few-Shot-Basic Google Few-Shot-Basic 216 ML 49.236443 9 0.0000001 0.9015634 0.8359391 0.1438672 0.1060731 0.1844139 0.0576465 ACCEPTABLE/POOR

Criterion: Truth 1-Factor Criterion: Truth 1-Factor 1222 ML 2.978265 2 0.3005049 0.9990994 0.9978962 0.0167670 0.0000000 0.0617330 0.0077296 GOOD/GOOD
