Table 4. CFA fit summary across all models. CFI = comparative fit index; TLI = Tucker-Lewis index; RMSEA = root mean square error of approximation with 90% CI; SRMR = standardized root mean square residual. Verdicts: CFI/RMSEA classification using Hu and Bentler (1999) cutoffs.

6 Executive Summary

comp <- merge(
  rel_df[, c("Scale", "n", "alpha", "omega_tot", "mean_IIC")],
  efa_df[, c("Scale", "n_factors_parallel")],
  by = "Scale", all = TRUE)

cfa_1f <- cfa_df[!grepl("2-Factor|3-Factor", cfa_df$Model), ]
cfa_1f$Scale <- cfa_1f$Model
comp <- merge(comp, cfa_1f[, c("Scale", "CFI", "RMSEA")],
              by = "Scale", all.x = TRUE)

comp$alpha     <- round(comp$alpha, 3)
comp$omega_tot <- round(comp$omega_tot, 3)
comp$mean_IIC  <- round(comp$mean_IIC, 3)
comp$CFI       <- round(comp$CFI, 3)
comp$RMSEA     <- round(comp$RMSEA, 3)

comp
Scale n alpha omega_tot mean_IIC n_factors_parallel CFI RMSEA
Criterion: Full 18-item 1222 0.903 0.908 0.350 4 NA NA
Criterion: Interaction 1222 0.863 0.870 0.374 3 NA NA
Criterion: Resolution 1222 0.858 0.867 0.672 1 NA NA
Criterion: Truth 1222 0.788 0.792 0.484 1 NA NA
Google Few-Shot-Advanced 217 0.922 0.924 0.665 2 0.895 0.235
Google Few-Shot-Basic 216 0.817 0.821 0.432 3 0.902 0.144
Google Zero-Shot 185 0.757 0.805 0.374 2 0.929 0.127
GPT5 Few-Shot-Advanced 216 0.901 0.905 0.610 2 0.942 0.154
GPT5 Few-Shot-Basic 186 0.878 0.882 0.549 1 0.986 0.067
GPT5 Zero-Shot 202 0.853 0.854 0.492 2 0.764 0.278

Table 5. Executive summary integrating key psychometric indicators across all scales. Alpha = Cronbach’s alpha; Omega = McDonald’s omega total; Mean IIC = mean inter-item correlation; n factors = number of factors retained by parallel analysis; CFI and RMSEA from one-factor CFA models (where applicable).

Table 5 synthesizes the psychometric evidence across all four analytic stages. The AI-generated scales produced by GPT-5 and Google Gemini Flash 2.5 generally demonstrated adequate to good psychometric properties across multiple indicators. Internal consistency estimates (alpha and omega) met or exceeded conventional thresholds for research use (≥ .70) in most conditions. Parallel analysis consistently supported a one-factor structure for the AI-generated scales (with the exception of conditions affected by the reverse-coded item), and one-factor CFA models showed acceptable to good fit for most scales. The criterion subscales, analysed via multiple imputation, provided a psychometrically sound benchmark for comparison.

The most salient finding across all analyses was the systematic underperformance of the reverse-coded item (Res_Item3_R) in the Google Zero-Shot condition. This item exhibited near-zero inter-item correlations, a low corrected item-total correlation, a weak factor loading, and contributed to the emergence of an artifactual second factor. These convergent findings across independent analyses provide strong evidence that this item does not function as intended and should be considered for removal.

In Simple Terms. This summary table brings together the most important numbers from every analysis in one place. For each scale, you can see at a glance whether the items are well-correlated (Mean IIC), whether the scale is reliable (alpha and omega), whether the items form a single factor (n factors = 1 is ideal), and whether that single-factor structure was confirmed (high CFI and low RMSEA). Most AI-generated scales performed comparably to the established criterion scale, which is a promising result for the use of large language models in test development. The Google Zero-Shot scale with the reverse-coded item is the clear exception.

Implications. These findings have three key implications for the emerging field of AI-assisted psychometric scale development. First, LLMs can generate psychometrically sound item sets when given appropriate prompting strategies (particularly Few-Shot approaches), supporting the viability of automatic item generation as a scalable tool for measurement research. Second, reverse-coded items generated by LLMs appear to be particularly prone to psychometric dysfunction, suggesting that AIG pipelines should either avoid reverse-coded generation or subject such items to rigorous pre-testing. Third, the comparability of AI-generated and human-authored scales across multiple psychometric indicators provides encouraging evidence for the broader adoption of AI-augmented test development workflows, with the important caveat that human expert review remains essential for quality assurance.

7 Conclusion and Discussion

The present psychometric evaluation examined six AI-generated Resolution scales — produced by crossing two large language models (GPT-5 and Google Gemini Flash 2.5) with three prompting strategies (Zero-Shot, Few-Shot-Basic, Few-Shot-Advanced) — alongside the Zhang et al. (2025) criterion measure of trust in AI. Across inter-item correlation analysis, reliability estimation, exploratory factor analysis, and confirmatory factor analysis, the majority of AI-generated scales exhibited psychometric properties that were broadly comparable to the human-authored criterion subscales. Internal consistency estimates (Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega) met or exceeded the .70 threshold conventionally required for research instruments in most conditions. Parallel analysis consistently supported unidimensional factor structures, and one-factor CFA models demonstrated acceptable to good fit for five of the six AI-generated scales. These converging results indicate that LLMs, particularly when guided by few-shot prompting strategies that incorporate example items and construct definitions, can produce coherent and internally consistent item sets suitable for measuring psychological constructs such as resolution in trust toward AI.

The most notable exception to this pattern was the Google Zero-Shot scale, which included a reverse-coded item (Res_Item3_R) that systematically underperformed across every analytic stage. This item produced near-zero inter-item correlations, a low corrected item-total correlation, a weak factor loading in both EFA and CFA, and drove the emergence of an artifactual second factor in the parallel analysis. The consistency of this finding across independent psychometric criteria provides strong evidence that the reverse-coded item did not function as intended, likely reflecting either a method effect associated with reverse-coded wording or a substantive misalignment between the AI-generated reverse-coded content and the remaining positively worded items. This result aligns with the broader psychometric literature cautioning against reverse-worded items in short scales (e.g., Weijters et al., 2013) and extends it to the domain of automatic item generation, where the risk of construct-irrelevant variance introduced by reverse-coding may be amplified by the tendency of LLMs to generate surface-level negations rather than genuinely conceptually opposite statements.

7.1 Limitations

Several methodological limitations should be acknowledged when interpreting the present results. First, the three-item criterion Resolution subscale was just-identified (df = 0) in CFA, yielding trivially perfect fit indices that provided no diagnostic information about model–data correspondence. Consequently, evidence for the structural validity of this subscale rested entirely on the IIC, reliability, and EFA results rather than on confirmatory fit indices, which limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn about the criterion’s factor structure at the subscale level.

Second, all psychometric evaluations were conducted within a single data collection, and the sample was drawn from one population of respondents. Without cross-validation on an independent sample, the stability of the factor solutions, reliability estimates, and CFA fit indices remains uncertain. Capitalization on chance in EFA factor extraction and CFA model evaluation is a recognized concern in single-sample psychometric studies (MacCallum et al., 1999), and replication is needed before strong conclusions about the generalizability of these findings can be drawn.

Third, the analyses focused exclusively on internal psychometric properties — dimensionality, internal consistency, and factor structure. Convergent and discriminant validity with external criteria (e.g., behavioural measures of trust, related but distinct constructs such as perceived competence or anthropomorphism) were not assessed. A scale can exhibit strong internal structure while failing to predict theoretically relevant outcomes, so the present results provide necessary but not sufficient evidence for construct validity.

Fourth, the criterion scale analyses relied on multiple imputation (m = 20) with Rubin’s rules. Although the low FMI values and stable alpha estimates across imputations suggest that the imputation model was adequate, the results remain conditional on the assumption that the data were missing at random (MAR). If the missingness mechanism was not at random (MNAR), the pooled estimates could be biased in ways that are not detectable from the observed data alone.

Fifth, measurement invariance across the six experimental conditions and across respondent subgroups (e.g., by gender, age, or prior experience with AI) was not tested. It is possible that items function differently across these groups, which would complicate direct comparisons of scale scores and psychometric properties between conditions.

Finally, only two LLM architectures were examined (GPT-5 and Google Gemini Flash 2.5), and only one target construct (resolution in trust toward AI) was considered. The generalizability of these psychometric findings to other LLMs, other psychological constructs, and other item formats (e.g., forced-choice, behavioural frequency) remains an open empirical question.

In sum, these findings support the viability of LLM-based automatic item generation as a scalable complement to traditional test development, with the important caveats that (a) reverse-coded items generated by AI require particular scrutiny, (b) few-shot prompting strategies that provide the model with example items and explicit construct definitions tend to produce more psychometrically sound item sets than zero-shot approaches, and (c) human expert review remains essential for ensuring the substantive validity and appropriateness of AI-generated content. The comparability of AI-generated and human-authored scales observed here is an encouraging finding for researchers seeking to leverage large language models in the measurement development process, though addressing the limitations outlined above — particularly cross-validation, external validity, and measurement invariance testing — will be essential for strengthening these conclusions.
